ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Italian seasonal mixed salad; 'salad food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for dressed mixed vegetable salads is the closest parent; no specific code exists for Italian seasonal salad.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Italian seasonal mixed salad; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Insalata di Stagione is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a full side or starter plate of mixed seasonal salad leaves with Italian dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Salad, vegetable, tossed, without dressing (USDA SR Legacy #11979) combined with Italian dressing (USDA SR Legacy #04119) at approximately 85:15 ratio
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Insalata di Stagione' as a restaurant dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for tossed vegetable salad (FDC #11979) and Italian salad dressing (FDC #04119) at an 85:15 weight ratio, which is consistent with a lightly dressed mixed salad. This is the highest-priority approved source available for this Western/Italian dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable salad with oil-based dressing
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Insalata di Stagione. GI of 15 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed vegetable salad with oil-based dressing, which is the closest analogous food. Raw non-starchy vegetables and olive-oil-based dressings consistently produce very low GI values (range 10–20) in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (15 × 3.9 × 200/100) / 100 = 1.17, rounded to 1.2.

hypertension.guidance: Insalata di Stagione contains approximately 148 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from the Italian dressing; individuals with hypertension should request dressing on the side or choose a reduced-sodium vinaigrette to keep a 200 g serving below 300 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 3.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Insalata di Stagione produces a negligible glycemic load and is well-suited as a starter or side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting postprandial blood glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil-based dressing providing monounsaturated fats and antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil component of the Italian dressing contributes monounsaturated fatty acids (approximately 2.4 g per 100 g) and the mixed seasonal vegetables provide folate, vitamin C, and vitamin E, all of which support cardiovascular health; this dish is consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Insalata di Stagione, meaning 'seasonal salad' in Italian, is a classic Italian mixed salad composed of fresh seasonal vegetables such as mixed salad leaves, tomatoes, cucumber, and red onion, dressed with a traditional Italian vinaigrette of extra-virgin olive oil, wine vinegar, and dried herbs. The dish is served raw and minimally processed, preserving the natural crunch and freshness of the vegetables. The flavour profile is light, crisp, and slightly tangy from the vinaigrette, with a clean finish characteristic of Italian antipasto or contorno (side dish) culture. Nutritionally, the salad is low in calories (approximately 52 kcal per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (3.9 g per 100 g), and provides a modest amount of healthy monounsaturated fat from olive oil. It is a good source of dietary fibre (1.4 g per 100 g), vitamin C, vitamin A, folate, and vitamin E. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content is moderate due to the dressing and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore and is priced at approximately SGD 17.99 per serving. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal diets. The dish aligns with the Mediterranean dietary pattern, which is associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and all-cause mortality.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Insalata di Stagione exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for tossed vegetable salad and Italian dressing at an 85:15 ratio, which is a defensible approximation. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables', which is the correct classification for a mixed salad dish. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC #11979 (tossed vegetable salad) and FDC #04119 (Italian salad dressing) at an 85:15 weight ratio. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable salad with oil-based dressing; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as olive-oil-based Italian dressing contains no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as raw vegetables and olive oil do not contain meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data. 9. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. No mandatory non-English local names are required under the Veritas multilingual matrix for Italian cuisine. 10. Allergen status: standard Italian dressing may contain sulphites in some commercial formulations; restaurant-specific allergen information should be verified directly with Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore.
